coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of carbon dots embedded zincone microspheres for luminescent detection and removal of dichromate anions in water
coredata.dc:creator: Xue, Daxiang
Yu, Fengjiao
Zhang, Zhijie
Yang, Yang
coredata.dc:description: Hexavalent chromium [Cr(VI)] is a carcinogenic and toxic pollutant and has brought severe environmental risks. Therefore, detection and removal of chromium ions with sensitive and economic materials are highly desirable. In this work, we develop a facile one-step synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) embedded zincone microspheres (CDs/zincone), which can act as low-cost and highly selective sensors for Cr(VI) based on the inner filter effect (IFE). A wide concentration linear range of dichromate ions (Cr2O7 2–) from 1 to 1000 μM can be detected by a small dose of the powders, and the detection process can be accomplished within 150 s. In addition, the CDs/zincone can also demonstrate efficient Cr2O7 2– removal with an uptake capacity of 47.5 mg/g. The in situ hydrolysis of zincone is shown to play a key role in the sensing and uptake of Cr2O7 2–. This CDs/zincone composite is the first bifunctional zincone material reported for both detection and removal of Cr2O7 2–. Moreover, hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA) is revealed as a new source for CDs in our system. This novel hybrid CDs/zincone material, which can be easily obtained by environmentally friendly synthesis, has potential applications for Cr(VI) detection and water treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Zincone
Luminescence
Inner filter effect (IFE)
Chromium(VI)

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Removal of K2Cr2O7 from aqueous solutions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the CDs/zincone microspheres for detection and removal of Cr2O7 2– ions in aqueous solutions.

(a) Optical microscope image of the as-prepared specimen. SEM images of the CDs/zincone displaying (b) a uniform spherical morphology and (c) a rough surface due to the electron beam irradiation. (d) XRD pattern of the CDs/zincone. The vertical lines indicate wurtzite ZnO phase (JCPDS no. 36-1451).

(a) TEM image of a CDs/zincone microsphere. (b) General (inset) and enlarged views on the surface area of a CDs/zincone microsphere showing the embedded CDs. The individual CDs are marked by red circles. TEM images of the microspheres before (c) and after (d) a long time electron beam irradiation. (e) TEM image of the shell of a hollowed microsphere in (d). Inset is an HRTEM image of ZnO nanoparticles generated by electron beam irradiation. (f) TG–DSC plots of the CDs/zincone product in air atmosphere (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

(a) Photo of the CDs/zincone reaction mixture under ambient light (left); photos of the residual suspension (right top) and the separated CDs/zincone precipitates (right bottom) under ambient and UV light. Photos of the products prepared in the presence of only Zn(Ac)2·2H2O (b) and only HMTA (c). Photoluminescent spectra of the CDs solution (d) and the CDs/zincone solution (e) under different excitation wavelengths.

UV–vis absorption, fluorescence emission and fluorescence excitation spectra of the CDs solution (a) and the CDs/zincone solution (b). Black curve in each figure refers to the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cr2O7 2– with a concentration of 35 ppm. Emission spectra of the CDs solution (c) and the CDs/zincone solutions (d) in the absence and presence of Cr2O7 2– with a concentration of 100 μM.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs/zincone solution exposed to different concentrations of Cr2O7 2– ions. (b) The correlation between the luminescence intensity monitored at 468 nm and the Cr2O7 2– concentration; inset is the Stern–Volmer plot of the CDs/zincone quenched by Cr2O7 2– aqueous solution.

Selectivity analysis of the CDs/zincone sensor for Cr2O7 2– over other ions and phenol by monitoring the quenching rate. The concentration of various ions and phenol was 100 μM. Error bar: standard deviation calculated from the results of three parallel measurements.

(a) Adsorption values of Cr2O7 2– over time (qt ) at C0 of 35 ppm by the as-prepared CDs/zincone powder, and N2- and air-calcined samples. Inset is photos showing color changes of the Cr2O7 2– aqueous solution due to adsorption by the CDs/zincone powder over time. (b) SEM image of the CDs/zincone microspheres after uptake of Cr2O7 2– ions from water for 5 min and (c) corresponding elemental mapping images of Zn, O, C and Cr of a CDs/zincone microsphere. (d) Removal capacities of the CDs/zincone powder, and N2- and air-calcined samples with various C0 , where qe is the amount of Cr2O7 2– ions adsorbed under equilibrium. Inset is photos of the CDs/zincone powder separated after 4 h Cr2O7 2– removal process at C0 of 53 ppm. The left was taken under ambient light and the right was under UV light.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of carbon dots embedded zincone microspheres for luminescent detection and removal of dichromate anions in water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering, College of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 210009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hexavalent chromium [Cr(VI)] is a carcinogenic and toxic pollutant and has brought severe environmental risks. Therefore, detection and removal of chromium ions with sensitive and economic materials are highly desirable. In this work, we develop a facile one-step synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) embedded zincone microspheres (CDs/zincone), which can act as low-cost and highly selective sensors for Cr(VI) based on the inner filter effect (IFE). A wide concentration linear range of dichromate ions (Cr2O7 2–) from 1 to 1000 μM can be detected by a small dose of the powders, and the detection process can be accomplished within 150 s. In addition, the CDs/zincone can also demonstrate efficient Cr2O7 2– removal with an uptake capacity of 47.5 mg/g. The in situ hydrolysis of zincone is shown to play a key role in the sensing and uptake of Cr2O7 2–. This CDs/zincone composite is the first bifunctional zincone material reported for both detection and removal of Cr2O7 2–. Moreover, hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA) is revealed as a new source for CDs in our system. This novel hybrid CDs/zincone material, which can be easily obtained by environmentally friendly synthesis, has potential applications for Cr(VI) detection and water treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium, as a fatal toxicant, was extensively applied in electroplating, printing and leather tanning etc. owing to fast growing urbanization and industrialization. Hexavalent chromium is the most hazardous on account of its greater mobility, mutagenic and carcinogenic properties [1,2]. The concentration of chromium in drinking water is strictly controlled, for instance, the guideline recommended by Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) in US is 100 ppb and the World Health Organization (WHO) is 50 ppb [3]. Therefore, simple and low-cost detection and removal of chromium is extremely crucial.

Conventional Cr detection methods including atomic absorption spectroscopy [4], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry [5], and surface enhanced Raman scattering [6] require intensive or time-consuming operation and complicated sample pretreatment, which prompts research on spectrofluorimetry [7–16]. On the other hand, it is important to remove chromium prior to water waste discharge. Removal approaches by chemical precipitation, biosorption, electrochemical reduction and photocatalytic reactions have some disadvantages including expensive equipment and production of new waste [17–21]. Adsorption remains the most widely applied process and it is of great significance to develop low-cost materials as adsorbent.

With the boom of nanomaterials science, carbon dots (CDs), a novel carbonaceous nanomaterial with the size smaller than 10 nm, possess a series of captivating characteristics such as low cytotoxicity, splendid chemical inertness and high efficiency fluorescence [22–25]. Based on the inner filter effect (IFE), which is caused by the absorption of the exciting and/or emitted light by absorbers during the detection process, chromium detection can be achieved with the CDs through the fluorescence quenching [8–15]. For example, Sun et al. developed a CD-based nanosensor with citric acid as the carbon source and diethylenetriamine as the surface passivation agent. The obtained CDs can be used as IFE-based fluorescent chemosensors for the determination of Cr(VI) with a linear range of 0.01–50 μM [8]. Feng et al. designed Co2+-doped CDs for sensing Cr(VI) ions with a linear range from 5 to 125 μM [12]. Cai et al. developed N, S co-doped CDs using pomelo as the carbon source for the selective and sensitive detection of Cr(VI) with a good linear range from 1 to 40 μM [13].

Luminescent metal organic frameworks (MOFs), as a rising class of porous materials, provide another fluorescence platform for chromium sensing. However, anion Cr2O7 2– sensing still remains a challenge because cationic MOFs occupy an extremely minor portion in the whole family. A number of luminescent Ln-MOFs, like Eu- and Tb-MOFs, are regarded as excellent cationic sensing candidates. Li’s group designed Eu- and Tb-MOFs that were used as sensors for benzaldehyde in methanol, as well as Cr3+, CrO4 2–, and Cr2O7 2– in water. The detection limits of the Cr2O7 2– ion are respectively 1.0 and 2.1 μM for Eu- and Tb-MOFs [10]. Wang et al. reported Eu-MOF as a Cr(VI) sensor with a linear correlation between 0 and 300 ppm, and Eu-MOF reached a maximum removal capacity of about 9.7 mg (Cr)/g [11]. Zr-MOFs are also commonly used in Cr2O7 2– detection. Cao group prepared Zr-MOFs for effective sensing of Cr2O7 2– with a linear range from 1.8 to 340 μM [15]. Qian group designed cationic Zr-cluster based MOFs with a Cr2O7 2– uptake capacity of about 245 mg/g [16]. Apart from the luminescent MOFs, efforts have been devoted to studying CDs-embedded MOFs. For instance, Chen et al. encapsulated CDs in MOFs for the detection of Cu2+ ions [14]. However, MOFs-involved methods usually use either expensive metals like Zr or Ln(III) series metals, or expensive ligands and complicated synthesis processes. Most of the detection linearity ranges are relatively narrow. Seeking easy and cheap detection and removal methods as well as broad detection range is thus very essential and urgent.

In this work, we report the synthesis of CDs-embedded zincone microspheres by a one-step solvothermal method. The CDs/zincone material exhibits fast, selective and sensitive detection of Cr2O7 2– as well as a wide linear range from 1 to 1000 μM. Furthermore, it also achieves efficient removal of Cr2O7 2– with a maximum capacity of 47.5 mg/g. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first bifunctional zincone material reported for both detection and removal of Cr2O7 2–. The CDs/zincone material has advantages over other reported sensors: low cost, able to both detect and remove chromium, facile one-pot synthetic method and functional in aqueous media. Detailed studies of the CDs/zincone structures are carried out and the relevant factors and mechanism are discussed. We also for the first time demonstrate that hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA) acts as the source of CDs in the synthesis.

Zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O: Zn(Ac)2·2H2O) was purchased from Xilong chemical Co., Ltd. Hexamethylenetetramine (HMTA) and potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) were purchased from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ethylene glycol (C2H6O2: EG) was purchased from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Company. All of the reagents were of analytical grade and utilized without further purification. Deionized water was used throughout this work.

Surface morphologies of the samples were observed on a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S-4800 microscope) that is equipped with an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) for elemental analysis. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were conducted from 20 to 80° with a scan rate of 0.2° s–1, on a SmartLab diffractometer (Rigaku Corporation). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) investigations were carried out using a JEOL JEM-2010 microscope operating at 200 kV. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, Netzsch STA 409) of the product was performed at a heating rate of 10 °C·min–1 in the air atmosphere. Fluorescent spectra were measured on a RF-5301/PC spectro-fluorophotometer and emission maxima (λmax) were expressed in nanometers. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer. Inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) analysis was conducted using a Perkin Elmer Optima 7000DV instrument. N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms were collected on a Micrometrics ASAP 2020MC surface area and porosity analyzer at 77 K after the sample had been degassed in vacuum N2 at 80 °C for 7.5 h. Surface area and pore size distribution of the samples were calculated using the BET and Barrett–Joyner–Halender (BJH) methods, respectively. The surface chemical structure was conducted with Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (Nicolet 6700, USA) using the standard KBr pellets, where transmittance mode was employed within a wavelength of 4000 and 400 cm–1 at a resolution of 4 cm–1.

The carbon dots embedded zincone microspheres were prepared by a one-pot method. First, 0.329 g of Zn(Ac)2·2H2O and 0.210 g of HMTA were dissolved in 120 ml of EG. After sonication for 30 min, the transparent solution was stirred for 10 min and heated at 170 °C in an electric oven for 10 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the yellow-brown precipitate was collected by centrifugation, washed three times with anhydrous alcohol and dried at 60 °C overnight. The two control samples were prepared by using only Zn(Ac)2·2H2O or only HMTA in EG.

The CDs/zincone microspheres were dispersed in ethanol to form a powder emulsion with a concentration of 0.67 mg/ml. Then 2.94 mL of the emulsion was taken into a cuvette, followed by adding 0.06 mL of Cr2O7 2– aqueous solution of various concentrations ranging from 0.05 to 50 mM. The final volume of the mixed solution was 3 mL, leading to the concentration of K2Cr2O7 in the range of 1 to 1000 μM. After the mixed solutions were incubated for 150 s, the corresponding emission values at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Removal of K2Cr2O7 from aqueous solutions
ce:section.ce:para:
The aqueous Cr2O7 2– stock solution (106 ppm) was prepared by dissolving 60 mg of K2Cr2O7 in 200 ml water in a standard flask. K2Cr2O7 aqueous solutions of various concentrations (18, 35, 53, 71, and 88 ppm) were prepared through successive dilutions of the stock solution with deionized water. First, 50 mg of the as-prepared CDs/zincone powder, the N2-calcined powder (550 °C for 1 h) and the air-calcined powder (400 °C for 1 h) were respectively added to 30 ml of Cr(VI) (35 ppm) solution under stirring. The removal capacity (qe , mg/g) was calculated according to Eq. (1) [26].
(1)
q e
=
C 0
−
C e
V
m
where Co and Ce are the initial and equilibrium concentrations of Cr(VI) in the solution (mg/L), V is the volume of the solution (L), m is the weight of the sample (g). Next, to study the removal capacity qe , 30 ml of Cr(VI) with different concentrations (18, 35, 53, 71, 88, and 106 ppm) were respectively mixed with 50 mg of the three samples for 4 h. The powder in the resulting suspensions was separated by centrifugation. The concentrations of Cr(VI) in the remaining solutions were determined by a UV–vis spectrophotometer using the absorbance band of Cr2O7 2–at 372 nm. To confirm the precision of the concentrations measured by UV–vis spectroscopy, we also employed ICP-OES analysis to determine the concentration of Cr(VI) in the remaining solution (C0 of 53 ppm) for each sample.

Zincones denote a class of zinc alkoxide hybrid polymers, in which zinc ions are bound to alcohols such as ethylene glycol (EG) or glycerol with a composition approximated by (–Zn–O–R–O–)n [27]. In our experiment, CDs/zincone microspheres were one-step prepared by the reaction of Zn(AC)2·2H2O in EG solution in the presence of HMTA at an elevated temperature of 170 °C.

The as-prepared CDs/zincone powder displays yellow-brown color, consisting of perfect microspheres with a very uniform size as observed under the optical microscope (Fig. 1 a). SEM image in Fig. 1b further reveals the spherical morphology and an average size about 2 μm. The smooth surface of the microspheres gradually became rough under continuous irradiation of the electron beam. A close look at the damaged surface is displayed in Fig. 1c, where numerous small sink holes are visible. As shown in Fig. 1d, the XRD peaks of the CDs/zincone product are significantly broadened, implying that in the amorphous microspheres, some ZnO nanoparticles with low crystallinity and tiny sizes also exist. The broad peak located at ca. 23° (2θ) comes from the CDs in the microspheres [28].

In order to study the structure in more detail, TEM analysis of the CDs/zincone product was carried out. TEM image in Fig. 2 a shows that the CDs/zincone has a nearly ideal spherical geometry. Small particles are embedded in the spheres as shown in Fig. 2b, which are likely to be CDs according to our later discussions. After the electron beam irradiation for a long time, the microspheres gradually transformed in-situ from the uniformly solid (Fig. 2c) to yolk–shell structure with large voids (Fig. 2d). The formation of the large voids was analogous to the generation of the sink holes under SEM observation (Fig. 1c), which were due to the decomposition of zincone matrix under the electron beam irradiation. Besides, well-crystallized ZnO clusters were observed (Fig. 2e) due to the decomposition of zincone matrix that constitutes the spheres. It is recognized that amorphous zincones can be decomposed into ZnO via heating or a displacement or a sputtering damage mechanism under high-energy electron beam. Accordingly, gasification of volatiles left behind numerous void spaces. To further determine the components in the product, thermal analysis in air atmosphere was performed and the results are exhibited in Fig. 2f. The first step of weight loss of ∼7.1% from room temperature to 250 °C originated from the adsorbed water and organics of low boiling points. The second step took place at the temperature range from 250 to 500 °C, with a weight loss of 34.7%, consistent with the theoretical value of 35.2% weight loss resulting from zincone (–Zn–O−CH2−CH2–O–)n decomposition into ZnO [29]. Notably, the TG and DSC curves present a large weight loss in a narrow temperature range centered at 300 °C, accompanied with a huge exothermic process, which suggests that combustion reactions were involved in the thermolysis. The above results confirm that zincone is the main component of the microspheres.

After the CDs/zincone precipitates were largely separated from the dark brown slurry of the product (left in Fig. 3 a), we still obtained an upper suspension with a yellow color under ambient light (right top in Fig. 3a). The suspension that was further diluted 10 times by ethanol presented a strong blue fluorescence under the excitation of 365 nm (right top in Fig. 3a). Meanwhile, the CDs/zincone precipitates displayed a brown color and a bright yellow fluorescence under the same UV excitation (right bottom in Fig. 3a).

In order to clarify the origin of the fluorescence, we carried out the controlled experiments, in which the precursor was pure solvent EG, or the EG solution of only HMTA or only Zn(Ac)2·2H2O with the original concentration. After the pure EG was heat treated at 170 °C for 10 h, there was neither color change nor fluorescence under the UV light (Figure S1), indicating EG itself is of high chemical stability at temperatures below its boiling point. When Zn(Ac)2·2H2O was used as the only additive in the EG solvent, we obtained a light cream suspension (Fig. 3b). The fluorescence of the undiluted suspension was undertint (Fig. 3b), possibly caused by a small amount of ZnO [30]. In contrast, the product prepared with only HMTA in the EG solvent showed a shallow brown color (Fig. 3c). The suspension subsequent to 10 times dilution by ethanol presented typical CDs fluorescence– bright blue luminescence (Fig. 3c). This finding suggests that the CDs were produced from HMTA. The photoluminescent spectra of the CDs solution (Fig. 3d) indicate that the emission wavelength of the CDs is excitation-dependent in the excitation wavelength range of 345–525 nm, with the maximum emission at 445 nm under the excitation of 365 nm. This behavior is closely related to the particle sizes and surface states of CDs [13]. The variation tendency of the photoluminescent spectra of the CDs/zincone solution almost agrees with that of the CDs (Fig. 3e), which suggests that the surface states of the CDs embedded in zincone are well maintained. The above results reveal that the production of the CDs is due to the presence of HMTA, and the observed fluorescence originates from the CDs instead of zincone or ZnO.

In the intensively investigated hydrothermal synthesis of ZnO, HMTA is usually regarded as a pH buffer and hydroxyl source in water [31]. In our synthetic system, however, only a trace amount of water (0.045 vol%) from Zn(Ac)2·2H2O exists. Therefore, limited HMTA is expected to participate in the hydrolytic process for the production of ZnO, and the Zn2+ ions mainly react with EG to form zinc alkoxide. The superfluous HMTA is thus consumed as the source of CDs via thermal pyrolysis in the EG solution. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that HMTA is found to be the source producing CDs. Meanwhile, both the alkoxidation and hydrolysis of Zn2+ ions under current condition are thermodynamically slow, leading to controlled release of monomers in a mixture of zinc alkoxide and few ZnO in the bulk solution. Since the development of zinc alkoxide and ZnO crystals is equally based on Zn–O bonding interactions between the nuclei and the newly-added monomers, the coexistent ZnO monomer might disturb the crystallization of zinc alkoxide, initiating a loose zincone network with amorphous nature. The CDs derived from HMTA are simultaneously packed in the gradually formed microspheres. In this way, one-step synthesis of CDs embedded zincone microspheres is realized.

For effective fluorescent detection of Cr2O7 2– based on the inner filter effect (IFE), the absorption band of Cr2O7 2– needs to overlap with the excitation and/or emission band of the fluorophore. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the CDs exhibit significant absorption in the wavelength range less than 450 nm, which has been ascribed to the formation of multiple polyaromatic chromophores as well as the n−π* transition of CDs [25]. The excitation spectrum of the CDs solution has a band at 365 nm, compatible with the absorption feature. Under the excitation of 365 nm, the CDs exhibit a characteristic emission band centered at 468 nm. Moreover, distinct absorption bands of Cr2O7 2− are observed to locate at 360 nm and 440 nm, which extensively overlap the excitation and emission bands of the CDs. Cr2O7 2– can thus shield the excitation light for CDs as well as slightly absorb the CDs’ emission light, in which way efficient IFE takes place. From Fig. 4b we can validate that the CDs/zincone solution shows spectral behavior similar to the CDs solution. As expected, the fluorescence quenching was achieved for both the CDs and the CDs/zincone system upon the addition of a low level of Cr2O7 2− (Fig. 4c, d).

As the fluorescence emission of the highly-dispersed CDs/zincone microspheres can be quenched by Cr2O7 2– ions, we further used the as-prepared CDs/zincone powder to prepare the probe solution and performed quantitative detection of Cr2O7 2– in water. The mixed solution was incubated for 150 s and then excited at 365 nm for fluorescence detection. The Stern–Volmer equation:
(2)
I 0
I
= 1 +
K
S V
[ A ]
evaluates the luminescence quenching efficiency, where Ksv is the quenching constant (M–1), I0 is the original luminescence intensity of the CDs/zincone emulsion in ethanol, I is the luminescence intensity after addition of various concentrations of Cr2O7 2– solutions, and [A] represents the molarity of Cr2O7 2– solutions (μM) [32]. As shown in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence intensity decreases with the increasing concentration of Cr2O7 2– ions. The Stern–Volmer curve exhibits a good linear relationship of I0/I against the Cr2O7 2– concentration ranging from 0 to 1000 μM with the correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9974 (inset in 5b). The corresponding UV–vis absorption spectra of the detection solutions are displayed in Figure S2. There are no detectable changes of the absorbance of Cr2O7 2– ions until exposed to high concentrations. Since the change in the absorbance of the CDs under the role of Cr2O7 2–-derived IFE can transform exponentially into that of fluorescence intensity, the fluorescent CDs/zincone sensor is of high sensitivity toward the detection of Cr2O7 2– ions.

In order to examine the selectivity of the CDs/zincone system, different anions and cations including Cr2O7 2–, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cl–, and C2O4 2–, and even phenol were investigated by using the quenching rate, denoted as (I0–I)/I0 , as criterion. Each detection measurement was repeated three times for average values and standard errors. The results are shown in Fig. 6 , where Cr2O7 2– presents outstanding quenching effect over all the other ions. Especially the C2O4 2– anion almost has no effect, indicating the electrical charge of the analyte is not the merely factor responsible for the fluorescence quenching of the CDs/zincone. To further illuminate the origin of the selectivity of the CDs/zincone toward Cr2O7 2–, we also measured the UV–vis absorption of aqueous solutions of Cd2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and C2O4 2– ions with the same concentration (Figure S3). In contrast to Cr2O7 2–, all of them show negligible absorption during the wide UV–vis wavelength range, resulting in the low IFE effect. The above experiments reveal that the CDs/zincone microspheres have good selectivity for Cr2O7 2–, and are possible to act as a fluorescent probe to detect Cr2O7 2– pollution in real samples with little interference from other ions and organics.

In addition to detection, removal of the toxic Cr2O7 2– from contaminated water is crucial. We found that the CDs/zincone microspheres can not only act as a sensitive fluorescent probe to detect Cr2O7 2– ions, but function as an efficient sorbent to capture them from water. Fig. 7 a presents the Cr2O7 2– uptake performance of the as-prepared CDs/zincone powder, in which the residual Cr2O7 2− concentrations remaining in the aqueous solutions were determined by UV–vis absorption spectra at 372 nm. At an initial Cr2O7 2– concentration (C0 ) of 35 ppm, the CDs/zincone achieved 77.7% of Cr2O7 2– uptake within 5 min and attained 97.2% when the adsorption equilibrium was established. As illustrated in the inset of Fig. 7a, it took only 5 min to turn the yellowish supernatant colorless upon the adsorption by the CDs/zincone. Fig. 7b shows an SEM image of the CDs/zincone microspheres after the Cr2O7 2– ions were adsorbed. The spherical morphology was generally maintained, but numerous sink holes appeared on the microspheres surface. Energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) elemental mapping shown in Fig. 7c demonstrates that the elements Zn, O, C and Cr are uniformly distributed in the whole microsphere, confirming the enrichment of Cr2O7 2– ions in the host material CDs/zincone from the aqueous solution. For comparison, we annealed the CDs/zincone powder respectively in N2 atmosphere at 550 °C and in air at 400 °C. The CDs/zincone was converted to ZnO/C in N2 and to ZnO in air, both of which inherited the original spherical morphology. When these two samples were used as sorbents for the removal of Cr2O7 2– ions from water, the ZnO/C only captured about 3.3% within 5 min (58.4% under equilibrium) while the ZnO only captured 1.6% in the same duration (58.7% under equilibrium). Furthermore, both the calcined samples reached adsorption equilibrium much slower than the CDs/zincone sample.

We further investigated the Cr2O7 2– removal capacity of the CDs/zincone sample by changing C0 from 18 to 106 ppm in the aqueous solutions. As seen in Fig. 7d, the Cr2O7 2– removal capacity of the CDs/zincone increases with the increasing C0 . The maximum uptake capacity reaches 47.5 mg/g at C0 of 106 ppm, which lies in a high level in the reported literature on the adsorptive elimination of Cr2O7 2– ions from water [11,33–35]. In contrast, the other two calcined samples present considerably smaller Cr2O7 2– uptake capacities and both the values are less than 30.0 mg/g at C0 of 106 ppm. Besides, the highest Cr2O7 2– removal efficiency (over 98%) of the CDs/zincone was achieved at C0 of 53 ppm, as marked by the red circle in Fig. 7d. To verify the precision of the results, we additionally determined the concentration of Cr2O7 2– ions residual in the aqueous solutions by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) analysis. As listed in Figure S4, the ICP-OES results at the C0 of 53 ppm for all the three samples are well consistent with those obtained by the UV–vis absorption spectra. After the 4 h removal experiment at the C0 of 53 ppm, the CDs/zincone powder that was separated from the solution present a bright yellow color due to the sufficient uptake of Cr2O7 2– ions from water. Accordingly, the luminescence of the CDs/zincone powder was completely quenched via the IFE under the excitation of 365 nm (inset in Fig. 7d).

In order to investigate the reason for the high removal capacity of the CDs/zincone sample, we first conducted Brunner–Emmet–Teller (BET) measurement to determine its specific surface area (SBET). As shown in Figure S5a, the SBET of the as-prepared CDs/zincone sample is only 3.4 m2 g–1. This result implies that the as-prepared microspheres have a nonporous and compact microstructure, consistent with our TEM observations. However, subsequent to the Cr2O7 2– removal process for 4 h, this sample provides a high SBET of 40.7 m2 g–1 and a narrow pore size distribution centered at 3.9 nm (Figure S5b). Such a huge SBET change indicates that the final structure with high porosity and large surface area was generated in situ once the CDs/zincone powder was in contact with the aqueous solution of Cr2O7 2– ions. In fact, the rapid hydrolysis of zincone is expected to occur in an aqueous environment, which could lead to the formation of ZnO and the release of EG.

ZnO is known as an amphoteric oxide with an isoelectric point of 9.5 in water at 25 °C [36]. When the solution pH is lower than the isoelectric point, positively-charged ZnO is capable of adsorbing anions by electrostatic attraction which is beneficial to the desired uptake of anions. Since the pH in our removal system is around 6, the newly-exposed ZnO sites via the hydrolysis of the CDs/zincone microspheres in the Cr2O7 2– aqueous solutions can accumulate Cr2O7 2– with negative charges [14,23]. Figure S6 exhibits the FTIR spectra of the CDs/zincone samples before and after the Cr2O7 2– removal process. For the as-prepared CDs/zincone sample, a broad band occurring at 3410 cm–1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of OH groups linked with hydrogen bonds. The peak at 1633 cm–1 is ascribed to bending vibration of adsorbed water. The absorption bands within 1454–1353 cm–1 are owing to the EG molecule, whereas the peaks within 1071–884 cm–1 are arising from the CO and CC or CH stretching [29]. A strong absorption band at about 453 cm–1 indicates the existence of ZnO phase in the original sample [30], in agreement with the XRD result in Fig. 1d. After the Cr2O7 2– removal experiment, the EG-derived absorption bands at around 1086 and 1044 cm–1 significantly reduced, indicating the partial release of EG due to the hydrolysis of zincone. The efficient molecule exchange between EG and water could induce in situ generation of abundant tiny pores in the CDs/zincone microspheres, as evidenced in Fig. 7b and Figure S5b. The excellent Cr2O7 2– uptake capacity was thus achieved, attributable to the synergistic effect of strong electrostatic attraction, large surface area and enhanced capillary force. In contrast, the ZnO microspheres that were produced by directly annealing the CDs/zincone in air have a smaller SBET of 21.7 m2 g–1 and a larger average pore size of 10 nm (Figure S5c), resulting in the much weaker Cr2O7 2– uptake capacity.

On the other hand, the excellent Cr2O7 2– sensing capability of the CDs/zincone probe also benefits from the fast enrichment of Cr2O7 2– from solutions in the CDs/zincone microspheres, followed by the IFE-induced fluorescent quenching associated with the embedded CDs. The strong adsorption ability of the CDs/zincone toward Cr2O7 2– ions can facilitate the accumulation of the target analyte around the fluorescent CDs sites that are uniformly distributed in the host, and thus greatly amplify the sensing signal of the fluorescent probe and increase its selectivity over the interfering cations. This accumulation effect is also conducive to the wide linear response range of the CDs/zincone for the detection of Cr2O7 2– ions. Therefore, the CDs/zincone microspheres can act as a novel bifunctional material, capable of detecting and removing Cr2O7 2– ions from aqueous solutions. The possible mechanism we proposed is illustrated in Scheme 1 .

In summary, CDs embedded zincone hybrid microspheres were successfully prepared through a facile one-step method in the presence of HMTA. The CDs/zincone product exhibits both excellent luminescent sensing and superior uptake capacity of Cr2O7 2– in aqueous solutions, but at a much lower cost compared to the MOFs counterparts. The as-prepared CDs/zincone has been applied to develop a sensitive and highly selective sensor for Cr2O7 2– ion, with a wide response range from 1 to 1000 μM. It presents a good removal capacity Cr2O7 2– ion up to 47.5 mg/g. In this work, we also offer a new method to produce CDs from HMTA through facile solvothermal synthesis. It is envisioned that the CDs/zincone could be applied to detect and eliminate dichromate pollution in industrial water, foods and living organisms.
None.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by grants from the Key Programs of Educational Commission of Jiangsu Province (15KJA150005), a research grant of the State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering (ZK201714) and the Project of Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD). Financial support from “the Youth Thousand Talents Plan” of China and “the Shuang Chuang Plan” of Jiangsu province for Y. Y. is also gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.09.101.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

A porous Zr-cluster-based cationic metal–organic framework for highly efficient Cr2O7 2– removal in water
